exemplar.title: To what extent has the Greek âSavings 2021â subsidy program been successful in increasing energy efficiency and reducing CO2 emissions?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the Greek âSavings 2021â subsidy program been successful in increasing energy efficiency and reducing CO2 emissions?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, focused and repeatedly linked to the analysis.
The topic is communicated effectively with an appropriate economic context.
A generally relevant and coherent methodology is presented, combining primary EPC data with reputable secondary statistics.
weaknesses: The initial literature/background section is overly verbose and delays direct engagement with the RQ.
Sampling is limited to two firms in one region, and alternative methodological approaches are not considered.
Justification for the 2018â2022 nationalâlevel comparison lacks depth and leaves potential confounding factors insufficiently addressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Across the three strandsâtopic communication, research question clarity and methodologyâthe work sits squarely in the middle of the 3-4 band. Two of the strands align with the upper half of that band, and one (methodology) shows mixed informed selection. On balance, a criterion-A score of 4 accurately reflects a clear purpose and solid methodological planning tempered by representativeness and focus issues.

strengths: Source material is integrated into tables, diagrams and narrative in a way that supports analysis.
Economic concepts (externalities, subsidies, welfare loss) are applied with correct formulae and diagrams.
Terminology is mostly accurate and consistent, with correct use of LaTeX notation for key formulas.
weaknesses: Reliance on non-academic sources (e.g., Wikipedia, PlumbingInstantFix) undermines academic rigor.
Some background narrative drifts into general description rather than analytic application of concepts.
Minor inaccuracies in curve labeling and occasional imprecise economic language appear.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall in the 3-4 band, showing clear knowledge and mostly appropriate source application and terminology use. Occasional lapses prevent placement in the top band, so a mid-band score of 4 best captures the performance in criterion B.

strengths: A wide range of quantitative analyses (means, percentage changes, standard deviations) is conducted and clearly interpreted.
Comparative macroâanalysis (Greece vs. Portugal) is well justified and adds depth to the argument.
Arguments are structured logically, and the evaluation acknowledges key limitations (sampling bias, external shocks).
weaknesses: Causality between the subsidy and national consumption trends remains inferential rather than rigorously established.
Statistical treatment is purely descriptiveâno inferential testing or confidence intervals are attempted.
Final policy evaluation lacks numerical welfare loss calculations and deeper costâbenefit quantification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While some inferential depth is missing, the investigation presents a majority of research that is appropriate and clearly relevant, offers clear analysis with supported conclusions, and develops a reasoned argument with partly critical evaluation. These qualities place the work in the 7-9 band; an 8 reflects its solid analytical breadth and focused critique despite methodological constraints.

strengths: The report follows a conventional structure with numbered headings, a detailed table of contents and correctly formatted tables and figures.
It meets core layout requirements: word count, title page, pagination and a consistent bibliography style.
Mathematical notation and figure captions are generally clear and correctly placed.
weaknesses: Formatting inconsistencies arise from mixed use of bold/italics and occasional misaligned tables.
Long URLs sometimes break the layout, and figure captions are intermittently embedded rather than uniformly placed beneath graphics.
Minor repetition in section headings and inconsistent figure numbering detract slightly from overall professionalism.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is coherent and navigable, fulfilling the key conventions of structure and layout. However, formatting lapses and small inconsistencies prevent a top-band score. A criterion D score of 3 accurately reflects a clearly appropriate but not flawless presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Clarity** â The research question is explicitly presented in italics on the title page and again in the introduction: âTo what extent has the Greek âSavings 2021â subsidy programme been successful in increasing energy efficiency and reducing COâ emissions?â. Wording is precise and the intended evaluation (âto what extentâ) is evident.

**Connection to discussion** â Subsequent sections are organised around energy-consumption, emissions and welfare-economics analysis, echoing the question. However, a perfect alignment is not sustained: national-level data are included without a robust causal bridge to the subsidy, diluting focus. This slippage prevents the question from being **âeffectivelyâ** connected at all times.

Overall, the strand meets the descriptors for the 3-4 band, leaning towards the upper end because the question itself is well-framed, but some loss of focus keeps it below the top band.

**Strengths** â The student states the broad topic â the impact of Greeceâs âSavings 2021â subsidy on household energy efficiency and COâ emissions â in the title, introduction and research-question box. The social and economic context (climate-change pressures on Greece, relevance to IB Economics syllabus) is explained, demonstrating why the topic is **appropriate for an Economics EE**.

**Limitations** â Communication is occasionally verbose, and the introduction spends a full page on general climate-change background before reaching the specific focus. The scope is ambitious (two different welfare outcomes, multiple data sets) and later sections reveal difficulties in linking micro-level findings to national consumption trends, showing that the focus, while clear, is not always tightly maintained.

Taken together, the topic is âadequately communicated with a largely appropriate purpose and focus,â matching the 3-4 band, with performance nearer the top of that range.

**Effective use** â Primary certificates, Eurostat databases, government documents and economic texts are integrated into the narrative and into tables, graphs and diagrams. The sources selected are largely relevant to assessing energy efficiency and emissions, and the student shows how each is applied (e.g., EPC data for micro analysis; Eurostat for macro context).

**Gaps** â Reliance on Wikipedia for key definitions and omission of peer-reviewed academic studies weaken the authority of the research base. The translation of Greek documents is not always clear, and some figures (e.g., energy-class conversions) lack explicit referencing.

Overall, the material is "mostly relevant and appropriate" with occasional lapses, aligning with the 3-4 band; the depth of application justifies awarding the upper score of 4.

**Organisation** â The essay follows the conventional EE structure with clear sections, numbered headings and a detailed table of contents. Tables, figures and appendices are integrated and referenced, aiding navigation.

**Issues** â Some sections overlap (e.g., âEnergy efficiency subsidy programâ vs. earlier introduction), leading to minor repetition. The âEvaluatingâ section is detached from the main conclusion, and figure numbering is occasionally inconsistent. Nonetheless, required elements (abstract is missing but not assessed here; introduction, methodology, conclusion, bibliography) are present.

These features satisfy the descriptor of a "clearly appropriate structure" but fall short of exemplary coherence, warranting a score of 3 within the 3-4 band.

**Positives** â The essay respects the 4 000-word limit, includes a word count, provides a title page, page numbers, tables and figures with captions, and a complete bibliography in a consistent format. Mathematical notation is rendered in LaTeX, and most visuals are clear.

**Areas for improvement** â Occasional formatting inconsistencies appear (e.g., mixed use of bold/italics, some long URLs breaking layout, misaligned table borders). Figure captions are sometimes embedded in body text rather than placed below graphics. These issues detract from full professionalism but do not impede understanding.

Overall, layout conventions are "largely appropriate and correctly applied" but not flawless, placing the work in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

**Argumentation** â The essay follows a logical structure: theory of externalities â programme description â micro evidence â macro comparison â evaluation. The student reflects on sampling bias, regional limitations, and external shocks (COVID, energy prices), showing **some critical perspective**.

**Evaluation depth** â However, evaluation of alternative explanations and policy trade-offs is brief, and the final judgement relies heavily on descriptive comparison rather than rigorous costâbenefit analysis. The discussion of opportunity cost and transport/industry sectors is asserted rather than analysed.

Overall, the work demonstrates a "reasoned argument with partially critical evaluation," matching the 4-6 band but nearer its lower end; a score of 4 is justified.

**Evidence of analysis** â The student computes percentage changes, means and standard deviations, interprets supply-and-demand diagrams, and compares countries to address the RQ. Most conclusions (e.g., significant household-level efficiency gains, limited national impact) are supported by presented data and economic reasoning.

**Limitations** â Causality is not rigorously established: increases in national energy consumption are noted but not conclusively linked to exogenous factors. Statistical treatment remains basic; no confidence intervals or hypothesis testing are attempted, so depth is moderate rather than sophisticated.

Thus, the analysis is "clear with mostly supported conclusions" but falls short of "effective" depth throughout. This places it comfortably in the 4-6 band; a score of 5 is appropriate.

**Demonstrated knowledge** â The essay explains negative/positive externalities, subsidies, welfare loss and standard deviation competently, using correct diagrams and formulae. Links to syllabus sections (microeconomic market failure) are explicit. The narrative shows a solid grasp of energy-subsidy mechanics and climate-policy context.

**Limitations** â At times, explanations lapse into descriptive background (general climate-change effects) rather than analytical Economics discussion. The macroâmicro linkage (from household certificates to national trends) is not fully theorised, suggesting some conceptual gaps.

Given the clear but not consistently sophisticated treatment, the work meets the "clear and mostly coherent knowledge" standard, meriting a score of 4 in the 3-4 band.

**Breadth and relevance** â The primary EPC data, Eurostat statistics and policy documents are directly tied to assessing the subsidyâs impact. The student explains how each source serves the investigation and critically notes sampling limitations and pandemic distortions, indicating **generally effective selection**.

**Shortfalls** â The primary data set is small and geographically narrow, and the absence of alternative indicators (e.g., control group without subsidy) constrains robustness. Some secondary sources (Wikipedia, popular media) are of limited academic weight, diluting the overall quality.

Given that "a majority of the research is appropriate and clearly relevant" but not consistently rigorous, the work sits in the 4-6 band; a mid-level score of 5 reflects these mixed qualities.

**Positive features** â The student combines primary data (30 Energy Performance Certificates) with secondary statistics from Eurostat and other reputable bodies, and outlines how each data set will be used. Basic statistical tools (mean, % change, standard deviation) and standard micro-economic diagrams are employed and briefly justified. 

**Shortcomings** â The primary sample is limited to two firms in one region, raising representativeness issues that the student only partly addresses. No alternative methodologies (e.g., regression or control groups) are considered, and the rationale for choosing 2018â2022 national comparisons is only partially convincing. Citations are plentiful but source selection sometimes favours easily accessible websites over academic or governmental studies.

Consequently, the work demonstrates "generally relevant sources/methods with some evidence of informed selection", placing it solidly in the middle band; a score of 3 reflects these mixed strengths and weaknesses.

**Accuracy and consistency** â Technical terms such as MSC, MSB, deadweight loss, standard deviation, negative/positive externalities and subsidy incidence are used appropriately in text and diagrams. Mathematical notation is mostly correct, and calculations are shown.

**Weaknesses** â Minor lapses appear (e.g., confusing MSB and MSB curve labelling, occasional typographical errors, and an unexplained reference to âB+â as both demand and efficiency class). Such slips suggest that terminology, while largely sound, is not flawless or always sustained.

Therefore, the strand demonstrates "mostly accurate" terminology, fitting the 3-4 band; the breadth of correct usage supports awarding a 4.

annotations.comment: The report title is clearly stated at the very start, fulfilling the requirement for a descriptive and focused title page. This enhances immediate understanding of the investigationâs scope.
The research question is explicitly presented and precisely framed, indicating a clear evaluative focus (âto what extentâ) that aligns well with the intended economic assessment.
The word count is provided prominently, confirming adherence to the maximum word limit and demonstrating compliance with formatting guidelines.
The choice of Portugal as a benchmark is well justified with multiple socioeconomic criteria, enhancing the relevance of the comparative macro-analysis.
The programâs funding sources, EU context and climate objectives are clearly explained, showing strong integration of policy background with economic analysis.
Income category table is comprehensive, showing clear methodological planning in defining applicant eligibility criteria for the subsidy.
Eligibility descriptions lack evaluation of potential exclusion of households (e.g., non-homeowners), limiting critical reflection on policy design.
Including an example energy performance certificate form enriches methodological transparency by showing actual data fields used in the primary analysis.
The household energy consumption table displays detailed before-and-after data, directly supporting calculated percentage changes and facilitating micro-level analysis.
Presenting large raw tables without a preceding summary statistic obscures key patterns; a brief analytic summary would improve data interpretation.
The bar chart vividly illustrates before-and-after changes in energy consumption, making the scale and consistency of impacts visually accessible.
Average change formula is provided but the accompanying narrative omits clear definition of the variable $$x_i$$, which may confuse readers less familiar with notation.
The detailed table of contents follows a logical hierarchy of sections and subsections, improving navigation and reflecting a clear organizational structure.
The introduction devotes excessive space to general climate background before narrowing to Greeceâs subsidy program, weakening the initial focus and delaying engagement with the research question.
The use of general descriptive narrative occasionally overtakes analytical economic discussion, reducing depth in applying discipline-specific concepts early in the essay.
Relying on only two firms in a single region for primary data constrains representativeness; the methodology should address potential sampling bias more rigorously.
Frequent citations of Wikipedia for key definitions undermines the academic rigour of source material; peer-reviewed or official publications would strengthen authority.
The mean formula is correctly presented in LaTeX ($$\bar{x}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n x_i}{n}$$), demonstrating precise use of mathematical notation for analysis.
The statistical treatment stops at descriptive measures; absence of confidence intervals or hypothesis tests limits the depth of inferential analysis.
Including a standard deviation calculation ($$\sigma=\sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\mu)^2}{n}}$$) demonstrates an effort to assess data consistency beyond mean values.
The justification for comparing 2018 and 2022 does not fully control for external factors (e.g., weather variation); a more robust counterfactual analysis is needed.
Terminology is occasionally imprecise (e.g., âpositive or negatives costsâ), suggesting the need for clearer definitions of economic concepts to maintain academic precision.
The positive externalities figure effectively illustrates welfare gains, demonstrating sound application of microeconomic theory to the subsidy context.
Footnotes are consistently formatted and complete, demonstrating attention to citation detail and reliability of referenced sources.
The Eurostat pie chart effectively contextualizes the household sectorâs share of final energy consumption, supporting the justification for the subsidyâs focus.
Reference to an upcoming table of income groups creates expectation, yet the table appears on the next page; integrating them more fluidly would improve structure.
Reliance on a non-academic source (PlumbingInstantFix) for technical household system efficiency data weakens the authority and relevance of supporting material.
The negative externalities diagram is well integrated and accurately labeled, reinforcing the theoretical explanation with a clear visual representation.
The diagram discussion omits policy implications (e.g., tax versus subsidy), which would deepen the evaluation of government intervention options.
The expression âQ1 + Q1 â Q*â is confusing; clearer algebraic presentation and explanation are needed to avoid misinterpretation of quantitatively optimal outcomes.
The subsidy-shift diagram is accurately drawn, showing how supply-side incentives achieve the socially optimal quantity, reinforcing theoretical insights with policy analysis.
The analysis of deadweight loss from the subsidy is not quantified; including numerical welfare loss calculations would strengthen the policy evaluation.
Minimum energy class upgrade table clearly delineates target improvements, facilitating precise measurement of eligibility and program outcomes.
Listing eligible interventions is thorough but lacks comparative assessment of cost-effectiveness across upgrade options.
The extensive list of measures overwhelms the reader; summarizing key categories with examples could improve readability and focus.
